examples.title: Basic Exception Handling with try-except
examples.description: Demonstrates fundamental exception handling using try-except blocks to handle ZeroDivisionError and ValueError gracefully
examples.explanation: The try block contains code that might raise an exception. The except blocks catch specific exceptions and handle them. Multiple except blocks allow different responses to different error types. The final except catches any unhandled exceptions. When 10/2 is executed, it prints 5 normally. When dividing by zero, ZeroDivisionError is caught and a user-friendly message is displayed.

examples.title: Advanced Exception Handling with else, finally, and raise
examples.description: Demonstrates else block (runs if no exception), finally block (always executes), and raising custom exceptions
examples.explanation: The else block executes only when no exception occurs in the try block - useful for code that should run on success. The finally block always executes regardless of whether an exception occurred - ideal for cleanup operations like closing files or releasing resources. The raise statement allows throwing custom exceptions. This example creates a robust input validation loop that continues until valid input is received or user quits.

examples.title: Custom Exceptions and Context Managers
examples.description: Demonstrates creating custom exception classes, using context managers, and exception chaining for professional error handling
examples.explanation: Custom exceptions inherit from Exception and can carry additional data like the invalid age value. Context managers use __enter__ and __exit__ methods to ensure proper resource cleanup - the __exit__ method always runs even if an exception occurs, making it ideal for file handling, database connections, or network sockets. This example demonstrates professional-grade error handling patterns commonly used in production software, including custom error types with payload data and automatic resource management.
